content:
The **bias–variance decomposition** formalizes ideas from **classical statistics** — **squared bias** and **variance** of estimators appear in sampling theory long before modern ML.

**Hoerl and Kennard's ridge regression** (1970) explicitly traded increased **bias** for reduced **variance** via L2 shrinkage.

**Geman, Bienenstock, and Doursat** (1992) popularized the neural-network framing of bias vs variance in "Neural Networks and the Bias/Variance Dilemma."

**Hastie, Tibshirani, and Friedman's** *Elements of Statistical Learning* (2001) made the three-term expected error decomposition standard teaching material for practitioners.

Today the same language guides **model selection**, **regularization**, and **architecture scaling** — from shallow linear baselines to deep networks — with **underfitting** understood as the practical face of **high bias**.

timeline.label: Classical estimation theory (20th century)
timeline.description: Bias and variance of estimators studied in statistics — foundation for later ML decomposition.

timeline.label: Ridge regression (1970)
timeline.description: Hoerl & Kennard introduce deliberate bias via shrinkage to stabilize linear models.

timeline.label: Neural bias/variance paper (1992)
timeline.description: Geman et al. articulate the bias–variance dilemma for neural networks.

timeline.label: ESL textbook (2001)
timeline.description: Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman unify bias², variance, and irreducible error for supervised learning.

timeline.label: Modern ML practice (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Validation curves, regularization, and scaling laws operationalize bias management in production pipelines.
